info.title: Codo HTTP Server API
info.summary: JSON API exposed by bin/codo — example endpoints, live server stats, and a Todo CRUD API backed by the embedded B-tree storage engine.
info.description:
Codo is a from-scratch multi-threaded HTTP/1.1 server written in C11. **Error bodies are HTML, not JSON.** Every failure path goes through `send_error_response()`, which emits `text/html` shaped like `…

404 Not Found

Todo not found

…` and closes the connection (`Connection: close`). Only success responses carry JSON. Clients must branch on the status code rather than attempting to parse an error body as JSON. **CORS.** The `cors_middleware` runs ahead of every route. Any `OPTIONS` request — to any path, including ones that do not exist — is answered directly with `204 No Content` plus `Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS`, an `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` value reflected from the request's `Access-Control-Request-Headers` (defaulting to `Content-Type`), and `Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400`. Requests carrying an `Origin` header get `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` set from the `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN` env key (default `*`). Same-origin traffic is left untagged. **Method fallthrough.** Routes are matched on path *and* method. A request whose method is not registered for a path falls through to the static file handler: `GET`/`HEAD` then attempt a file lookup under `DOCUMENT_ROOT` (typically `404`), while any other method returns `405 Method Not Allowed`. So `GET /api/echo` yields `404`, but `PUT /api/hello` yields `405`. **TLS.** When `server.crt` and `server.key` exist in the working directory and `SSL_ENABLED` is not `false`, the same endpoints are served over HTTPS on the same port.

port.description: Listen port; set via the PORT env key or argv[1].

tags.name: Todos
tags.description: CRUD over the embedded B-tree storage engine (buffer pool, write-ahead log, transactions). Todos persist across restarts. These handlers are dispatched to the storage thread pool rather than running on the epoll worker loop.

get.description: Scans the whole B-tree inside a transaction and returns every todo as a JSON array. Ordering follows the B-tree key order — keys are the todo ids rendered as decimal strings, so the sort is lexicographic, not numeric (`"10"` sorts before `"9"`). The array is empty (`[]`) when no todos exist.

post.description: Creates a todo from the JSON body. `title` is required; `completed` defaults to `false` when absent or unparseable. Any `id` supplied in the body is **ignored** — the server assigns the next id from a process-wide atomic counter, seeded at startup from the highest id already on disk. The created object is returned in full.

requestBody.description: An empty or missing body is rejected with `400`.

201.description: Todo created and committed to the write-ahead log.

400.description: Body absent/empty, or `title` missing, not a JSON string, or 1024 bytes or longer.

text/html.example:
400 Bad Request

Missing or invalid 'title'

put.description:
Full replacement, not a partial patch: `title` is required, and an omitted `completed` resets the field to `false` rather than preserving its stored value. The id always comes from the path — an `id` in the body is ignored. The todo must already exist; this never upserts.

Internally the storage engine's `db_update` only handles same-length values, so the replacement is performed as a delete followed by an insert inside a single transaction, rolled back on failure so a committed delete can never orphan the todo.

400.description: Non-numeric id, absent/empty body, or missing/invalid `title`.

text/html.example:
400 Bad Request

Missing or invalid 'title'

delete.description: Deletes the todo and returns an empty `204`. Deleting an id that does not exist is a `404`, not a no-op success.

get.description: Atomic counters held on the `http_server_t` instance.
Process-wide lock-free counters updated on the accept / read / write / close paths.

500.description: The formatted counters did not fit the response buffer.

text/html.example:
500 Internal Server Error

Stats too large

get.description: Returns a fixed JSON document. The `timestamp` is a hard-coded literal, not the current time.

timestamp.description: Hard-coded; does not reflect request time.

post.description: Returns the request body verbatim as `text/plain`, whatever its content type. An empty body echoes an empty string. Note that `GET /api/echo` is not routed and falls through to the static file handler, yielding `404`.

get.description:
Performs the RFC 6455 opening handshake: the request must carry `Upgrade: websocket`, `Connection: Upgrade`, and a `Sec-WebSocket-Key`. The server replies `101 Switching Protocols` with `Sec-WebSocket-Accept` computed as base64(SHA-1(key + GUID)), after which the connection leaves HTTP and speaks WebSocket frames.

A plain `GET` without the upgrade headers is rejected with `400`.

text/html.example:
400 Bad Request

WebSocket upgrade required

TodoId.name: id
TodoId.description: Decimal digits only. Anything else — a negative sign, a leading `+`, whitespace, or letters — is rejected with `400` before the storage engine is consulted.

Todo.description: A todo as stored and returned. Persisted in the B-tree keyed by its id rendered as a decimal string.

id.description: Assigned by the server. Never taken from a request body.

TodoInput.description:
Request body for create and replace.

**Size limits.** Two separate 1024-byte ceilings (`MAX_VALUE_SIZE`) apply, and they surface as different status codes. A decoded `title` of 1024 bytes or more overflows the parse buffer and is reported as `400 Missing or invalid 'title'` — the same code as a syntactically bad title. Below that, the *rendered* object `{"id":…,"title":"…","completed":…}` must itself fit 1024 bytes after JSON-escaping, or the request fails with `413 Todo too large`. For a plain-ASCII title the 413 band therefore begins around 986 characters, since the surrounding JSON costs roughly 38 bytes.

Escaping expands the title, lowering the 413 threshold further: each `"`, `\`, newline, tab, or carriage return costs two bytes in the stored form, and other control characters cost six (`\u00XX`).

**Encoding.** Raw UTF-8 bytes in the title are stored and returned unchanged. Only literal `\uXXXX` escape sequences are lossy — the parser collapses each to a single `?`, so `"caf\u00e9"` is stored as `caf?` while the raw bytes of `"café"` round-trip intact.

title.description: Required. Must be a JSON string; a number, `null`, or a missing key is a `400`.

completed.description: Optional. Absent or unparseable values are treated as `false`. On `PUT` this means omitting it resets a completed todo back to incomplete.

packets_sent.description: Counted as write() calls, not TCP segments.

packets_received.description: Counted as read() calls, not TCP segments.

HtmlError.description:
Error bodies are HTML, never JSON. Served as `text/html` with `Connection: close`. Each response supplies its own example; the shape is always `{status}

{status}

{message}

`.

text/html.example:
404 Not Found

Todo not found

InvalidTodoId.description: The path id was empty or contained non-digit characters.

text/html.example:
400 Bad Request

Invalid todo id

TodoTooLarge.description: The rendered todo exceeded the storage engine's 1024-byte value limit.

text/html.example:
413 Payload Too Large

Todo too large

500 Internal Server Error

Transaction failed
